subject: Suez Canal
executive_summary:
The Suez Canal, connecting the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea via Egypt, is one of the world's most critical maritime chokepoints, historically handling 12-15% of global trade and approximately 59-75 vessels per day. The canal has been severely disrupted since late 2023 by the Houthi Red Sea shipping crisis, and is now facing a second wave of uncertainty due to the March 2026 Hormuz crisis. Despite a cessation of Houthi attacks following the Gaza peace plan in October 2025, traffic remains approximately 60% below pre-crisis (2023) levels as of early 2026, with container shipping hit hardest at 86% below 2023 transits.

The Houthi campaign from November 2023 through September 2025 involved 99 attacks or hijackings of commercial vessels, disrupting approximately $1 trillion in goods. Suez Canal revenues plummeted from a historic high of $10.25 billion in 2023 to approximately $4 billion in 2024 — a 60%+ decline — severely impacting Egypt's fiscal health and contributing to a 0.6% GDP reduction. The Cape of Good Hope alternative route adds 10-14 days of transit time and $200-400 per TEU in additional costs, with freight rates between Shanghai and Rotterdam increasing 80%.

A tentative return to the canal began in late 2025, with Maersk's Sebarok becoming the first Maersk vessel to transit since early 2024 on December 19, 2025, and CMA CGM announcing service returns in January 2026. However, on February 28, 2026, the Houthis threatened to resume Red Sea attacks in response to the Iran war, casting uncertainty over the recovery. As of March 2026, the canal faces the dual threat of renewed Houthi activity and spillover from the Hormuz crisis, with the USS Gerald R. Ford carrier strike group transiting the canal on March 5 en route to the Persian Gulf.

key_findings.headline: Suez Canal traffic still 60% below pre-crisis levels despite 100+ days without Houthi attacks
key_findings.detail: Even after more than 100 days without a Houthi attack (following the October 2025 ceasefire), Suez Canal traffic remains approximately 60% lower than 2023 levels. Container shipping has been hit hardest, with Q4 2025 transits down 86% from 2023. Product tankers have been quickest to return, with Q4 2025 transits down only 19%. Monthly vessel transits fell from 2,068 in November 2023 to approximately 877 in October 2024.

key_findings.headline: Canal revenues collapsed from $10.25 billion to approximately $4 billion
key_findings.detail: Suez Canal revenues fell from a historic high of $10.25 billion in 2023 to approximately $4 billion in 2024, with projections of further decline to $3.6 billion in FY 2024/2025. This 60%+ revenue decline has severely impacted Egypt's fiscal position, contributing to a 0.6% dip in GDP as tax revenues linked to the canal were cut by more than half.

key_findings.headline: Houthi threats to resume attacks create new uncertainty for recovery
key_findings.detail: On February 28, 2026, in response to the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, the Houthis threatened to escalate the conflict and resume Red Sea attacks. While they have so far avoided resuming hostilities (not wanting to open multiple fronts), the threat alone has dampened carrier willingness to return to the Suez route and keeps insurance premiums elevated.

key_findings.headline: Cape of Good Hope rerouting adds 10-14 days and $200-400 per TEU
key_findings.detail: Ships rerouting via the Cape of Good Hope face significantly longer journeys, adding 10-14 days of transit time plus higher inventory costs and sustained freight premiums of 25-35%. Additional fuel, crew, and operational costs amount to $200-400 per TEU. Tankers burned an estimated 200,000 barrels/day extra fuel oil in 2024 due to the diversion. Container ship transits through the Suez plummeted 90% between December 2023 and March 2024.

key_findings.headline: 99 Houthi attacks over 22 months disrupted $1 trillion in trade
key_findings.detail: Between November 2023 and September 2025, the Houthis attacked or hijacked ships 99 times, with the final incident in September 2025. The campaign disrupted approximately $1 trillion in goods from October 2023 to May 2024 alone. The attacks killed multiple seafarers and sank commercial vessels, fundamentally altering global shipping patterns.

key_findings.headline: Major carriers tentatively returning but full recovery uncertain
key_findings.detail: CMA CGM announced MEDEX and INDAMEX services would return to Suez Canal routing in January 2026. Maersk's Sebarok became the first Maersk vessel to transit since early 2024 on December 19, 2025. However, many carriers continue to route via the Cape of Good Hope due to persistent security concerns and the new threat from the Hormuz crisis.

recent_developments.headline: USS Gerald R. Ford carrier strike group transits Suez Canal
recent_developments.summary: The Gerald R. Ford Carrier Strike Group transited the Suez Canal on March 5, 2026, en route to the Persian Gulf to join the Abraham Lincoln CSG amid the Hormuz crisis. The military transit underscores the canal's continued strategic importance even as commercial traffic remains depressed.
recent_developments.impact: Demonstrates canal remains operationally open for military transit; highlights Egypt's strategic role as transit facilitator; commercial recovery complicated by military escalation in the region.

recent_developments.headline: Houthis threaten to resume Red Sea attacks amid Iran war
recent_developments.summary: Following the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, the Houthis threatened to escalate and resume attacks in the Red Sea in solidarity with Iran. While they have so far avoided resuming hostilities, the threat has chilled the tentative recovery of Suez Canal commercial traffic that began in late 2025.
recent_developments.impact: Reverses momentum toward Suez Canal traffic recovery; keeps insurance premiums elevated; carriers pausing return-to-Suez decisions.

recent_developments.headline: First Maersk vessel transits Suez Canal since early 2024
recent_developments.summary: Maersk Sebarok became the first vessel from the world's second-largest container shipping line to transit the Suez Canal since the company suspended Suez routing in early 2024 due to Houthi attacks. CMA CGM also announced service returns for January 2026, signaling cautious industry re-engagement with the route.
recent_developments.impact: Positive signal for canal recovery; however, traffic remains 60% below 2023 levels; recovery pace depends on sustained security.

recent_developments.headline: Houthis halt attacks following Gaza peace plan implementation
recent_developments.summary: The Houthis halted attacks on international shipping and Israel after the Gaza peace plan went into effect on October 10, 2025, ending a 22-month campaign of 99 attacks. The ceasefire created conditions for tentative commercial recovery, though shipping industry confidence remained fragile.
recent_developments.impact: Ended the active attack phase; however, structural damage to Suez routing confidence persists; 60% traffic deficit continues months later.

risk_assessment.current_risk: The Suez Canal faces a precarious dual-threat environment. While the Houthi ceasefire (October 2025) ended the active attack phase, traffic remains 60% below pre-crisis levels and the tentative recovery has been halted by Houthi threats to resume attacks in response to the March 2026 Iran war. The canal itself is operational, but the Red Sea approaches via the Bab el-Mandeb remain a contested zone. Egypt faces severe fiscal strain from the revenue collapse ($10.25B to ~$4B). The probability of a return to pre-2023 traffic levels in 2026 is low given the compounding geopolitical risks.
key_drivers: Houthi threats to resume Red Sea attacks in response to Iran war (Feb 28, 2026)
Hormuz crisis creating cascading regional instability affecting Red Sea security
Persistent carrier reluctance to return despite 100+ days without attacks
Container shipping transits still 86% below 2023 levels
Insurance premium uncertainty for Red Sea transit
Egypt's fiscal dependence on canal revenues creating pressure for security guarantees
Proven Houthi capability to strike commercial vessels with missiles, drones, and small boats
mitigation_actions: Operation Prosperity Guardian (U.S.-led) continuing naval patrols in Red Sea approaches
Combined Task Force 153 maintaining Red Sea maritime security operations
Gradual carrier return (CMA CGM, Maersk) testing security conditions
Egypt coordinating with U.S. and regional partners on canal security
Industry adaptation to Cape of Good Hope routing reducing single-point vulnerability
Enhanced vessel self-protection measures and convoy procedures
